From the top view, the tab now shows a visible rounded-rectangle recess with a step around its perimeter. The bib's bottom-edge silhouette is clearly readable in the thumbnail.

## Fix 2 — taper the contour depth from zero at the bend

Current v4: the V-groove on the flap starts abruptly at the bend line. The dish depth is constant along the flap.

Target: the dish depth **varies** along the flap's length — zero at the bend (flap_start), ramping linearly (or smoothly) to `contour_depth` at the flap's front edge (the drip edge).

### Geometry approaches — pick one that produces a clean manifold

**A. Subtract a CONE (tapered cylinder) instead of a constant-radius cylinder.**
   The cylinder used to dish the flap becomes a cone whose radius ramps from ~0 at the bend-end of the flap to the full dish radius at the front edge. This naturally produces zero dish at the bend and full dish at the drip edge. Cone axis parallel to the width axis (X), cone's generating profile aligned with the flap's length axis.

**B. Hull() two dish shapes at the endpoints.**
   One shape at the bend with near-zero subtraction volume; one at the front edge with full subtraction. Hull produces a smooth interpolation.

**C. Rotate the subtracted cylinder's axis.**
   Keep the cylinder shape but tilt its axis so the subtraction depth increases linearly from bend to front. Requires careful geometry but works.

Prefer **A** — most deterministic manifold, easiest to reason about.

Keep the raised side rims on the flap (the lateral containment) as-is; this tweak is only about the depth profile along the flap's length.

## Scope

Modify `models/aquor_bib_drip_deflector.scad`:

- Add the three new `@param`s for bib recess.
- Implement the recess subtraction on the tab's top.
- Change the flap dish subtraction from constant-depth to tapered (option A preferred).
- Regenerate `PRINT_ANCHOR_BBOX` (recess will reduce volume slightly, but bbox likely unchanged).
- Update stock `@preset`.
- Update header comment explaining the recess and its alignment purpose.
- Regenerate thumbnails.

Keep everything else from v4:
- Uniform plate thickness (~2.5 mm) outside the recess
- Smooth bent-plate topology
- V-groove contour concept (just tapered now)
- Rounded tab rear corners
- Watertight + manifold + single connected solid
- Volume ≤ 15 cm³

## Acceptance

- Top view (or iso) clearly shows a rounded-rectangle recess on the tab's top-rear. Outline matches the bib face-plate's bottom edge profile.
- Side view shows the flap's top surface (centerline) starting FLAT at the bend and progressively deepening toward the front edge — no sharp onset at the bend.
- Iso render reads as "smooth continuous bend with a tapered channel growing into the drip edge" — no visible crease or step.
- `check-invariants` exits 0. Volume stays ≤ 15 cm³ (may decrease slightly vs v4's 6.94 cm³ due to the recess; that's fine).
- Orphan-fragment invariant passes — watch for CSG artifacts where the recess meets the tab's perimeter or where the tapered cone subtraction grazes the flap's side rims.
- E2E + vitest + pytest green.
- Commit trailer present. Thumbnails regenerated + committed.

## Do not

- Cut the recess deeper than `top_thickness - 0.3 mm` — never go through the plate.
- Move the recess — it's at the REAR of the tab, against the wall side. The front portion of the tab stays flat/unrecessed as visual frame.
- Regress the v4 smooth bend (geometry around the bend is fine; only the contour DEPTH along the flap changes).
- Adjust the flap's side rims. Those stay as-is.
- Change the catalog entry, slug, or category.
- Use `path_sweep` for the tapered cone unless you verify watertight passes on this geometry.

## References

- User preview v4 with feedback: `/tmp/deflector-v3.png` (previous iteration's issue commentary) and the current v4 thumbnails at `renders/aquor_bib_drip_deflector/{iso,top,side,front}.png`
- Bib dimensions: `/tmp/aquor-bib-dimensions.png` — use these for the recess outline
- Original user sketch: `/tmp/aquor-user-sketch.jpg`
- Previous revise beads: st-s7b, st-2ln, st-r38, st-if3 — avoid re-introducing any of the earlier issues (solid block, two-rectangle look, etc.)
- BOSL2 helpers: `libs/BOSL2/shapes3d.scad` (cone, cyl), `libs/BOSL2/shapes2d.scad` (rect with rounding)
- Invariants: `scripts/check-invariants.py`, `models/aquor_bib_drip_deflector.invariants.py`

title: [revise-v4] Aquor deflector: larger bend radius for smoother tab→flap blend + tab outline matches bib's rounded-rectangle bottom
description:
attached_molecule: st-wisp-osw4
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-04-24T13:50:38Z
attached_vars: ["base_branch=main"]
dispatched_by: mayor
formula_vars: base_branch=main

## Context

v3 landed (st-r38, commit likely latest on main — check `git log --oneline -1 -- models/aquor_bib_drip_deflector.scad`). The smooth bent plate + concave V-groove contour is the right direction. Two remaining issues from user review of the preview:

1. **Blend the tab→flap transition better.** In the v3 render (`/tmp/deflector-v3.png`) the bend between tab and flap is visible as a distinct crease line across the top surface. The concave V-groove abruptly begins AT that crease. Feels like a visible fold, not a smooth bend.
2. **Tab outline should match the bib's bottom edge.** The Aquor face plate is a rounded-rectangle (see `/tmp/aquor-bib-dimensions.png`) with ~8–10 mm rounded corners. Currently the tab is a plain rectangle — its top corners don't match the bib's bottom curves. When installed, there's a visual break at each corner between the bib's curved bottom and the tab's sharp corner. The tab's PERIMETER (top-down view) should be a rounded-rectangle profile whose top edge + rounded corners align with the bib's bottom outline.

## Fix 1 — smoother bend

Two changes compose:

### A. Larger bend radius

Bump `bend_radius` default from ~6 mm to ~12 mm. Expose a sensible range (`min=4 max=20`). Larger radius = longer arc length in the transition = the crease looks less like a fold and more like a bend.

### B. Contour transitions into the bend region

Currently the V-groove starts at the bend (first section of the flap). That creates a sharp onset at the fold. Options, pick whichever produces a cleaner manifold:

- **Taper the contour depth across the bend.** From 0 mm at the start of the bend arc (where it meets the tab) to `contour_depth` (the full dish depth) a few mm into the flap. A `linear_extrude` of a varying-depth dish isn't directly available in OpenSCAD; achievable by `hull()` of two endpoint shapes, or by subtracting a cone/wedge instead of a constant-radius cylinder so the subtraction depth varies smoothly along the flap's length.
- **Extend the V-groove geometry through the bend so the contour reads as a continuous channel from just-past-the-VHB-zone to the drip edge.** But the tab must stay flat (VHB), so the groove must start AT OR AFTER the bend — not before.
- **Shallower flap dish near the bend, deeper near the front edge.** Same effect as tapered-depth; biases water toward the outer edge anyway.

The key constraint: **tab top surface stays dead flat** (VHB contact zone). Contour only on the flap side of the bend. The goal is a smooth VISUAL transition from flat tab through the bend into the contoured flap.

## Fix 2 — tab outline matches bib's bottom

The tab is a horizontal shelf. Currently its top-down perimeter is a simple rectangle (width × tab_depth). New perimeter: a rounded-rectangle that matches the bib's face-plate outline where the tab sits against the bib underside.

### Geometry

- Add new `@param`s:
  - `bib_corner_radius` (default 9, mm, group=fit, min=0, max=20) — radius of the Aquor face plate's rounded corners. Measure from `/tmp/aquor-bib-dimensions.png` if possible; 8–10 mm is the typical Aquor spec.
- The **tab's top-rear profile** (viewed from above, tracing the outline where the tab contacts the bib underside): **rounded rectangle** of `width × tab_depth`, with the two REAR corners (against the wall) rounded at `bib_corner_radius`. The front edge + front corners remain straight (or stay rounded per the existing aesthetic).
- The **flap's rear edge** (where it meets the bend) inherits this rounded-rectangle width — so the flap is also narrower at the rear than middle. Visual effect: from the front, the part silhouettes into the bib's curves rather than butting up as a sharp rectangle.

**Implementation (option A, preferred):** use `offset()` on the 2D path (tab outline) with `r=bib_corner_radius` to round the rear corners, then linear-extrude the bent-plate profile through this varying-width path. If the polecat's existing implementation uses a static-width cross-section swept along a side-profile path, this may require restructuring to use a true 3D hull or an offset-extrude combo. Document the approach chosen.

**Implementation (option B, simpler):** keep the flap width constant; only round the tab's rear corners. The tab becomes a rounded-rectangle-topped slab, the flap extends from the tab's front edge at full width. Slightly less visually-continuous but dramatically simpler.

Pick B unless A is trivially expressible. The user's complaint is about the CORNERS at the tab's top (where the bib's curves live), not the flap silhouette. B solves that.

## Scope

Modify `models/aquor_bib_drip_deflector.scad`:
- Update `bend_radius` default to 12; keep it as a param.
- Apply one of the contour-transition approaches (A or B or shallower-near-bend) to blend the V-groove into the bend.
- Add `bib_corner_radius` param.
- Round the tab's REAR corners per option B (or optionally full rounded-rectangle per option A).
- Recompute `PRINT_ANCHOR_BBOX`.
- Update the stock `@preset` values.
- Update the header comment describing the rounded tab + softer bend.
- Regenerate thumbnails (`scripts/render-all.py`).

Keep everything else from v3:
- Uniform plate thickness (~2.5 mm)
- Concave V-groove contour on the flap (water to center)
- Volume ≤ 15 cm³ sanity invariant (may nudge up slightly if rounded tab adds a tiny bit; don't blow past 15)
- Watertight + manifold + single connected solid
- Printable flat-on-bed, no supports
- Catalog entry, slug, category unchanged

## Acceptance

- Iso + side thumbnails show the tab→flap blend as a smooth, continuous curve — no visible crease line. The V-groove should look like it "grows in" from the bend region rather than appearing abruptly.
- Top-down (or iso) thumbnail shows the tab with rounded rear corners matching the bib's face-plate curve.
- `check-invariants` exit 0. Orphan-fragment issue from the first v3 attempt does NOT regress — check watertight + orphan counts explicitly.
- Volume ≤ 15 cm³.
- All existing E2E + vitest + pytest green.
- Commit trailer present; thumbnails committed.

## Do not

- Introduce `path_sweep` for the bent-plate geometry unless the v3 implementation already uses it and the watertight invariants pass. The project has a specific path_sweep-watertight gotcha (st-hnd lineage).
- Break the VHB-zone flatness. Tab top surface must stay flat in every cross-section for VHB to bond. The contour only begins AT OR AFTER the bend.
- Revert the V-groove contour. Keep the water-to-centerline concave on the flap.
- Change the catalog entry, slug, or category.
- Allow invariant-failure commits to ship — if orphan fragments appear, rework the boolean ops (try `hull()`-based approach or buffer the subtraction bounding box by 0.1 mm to kill coplanar-face artifacts).

## References

- User preview showing the v3 result + feedback target: `/tmp/deflector-v3.png`
- Bib face-plate dimensions (for `bib_corner_radius`): `/tmp/aquor-bib-dimensions.png`
- User sketch (overall intent — still valid): `/tmp/aquor-user-sketch.jpg`
- Previous revision: st-r38 (v3, latest commit on main touching the model)
- BOSL2 rounding helpers: `libs/BOSL2/rounding.scad` (`round_corners`, `rect`, `offset`)
- Invariants: `scripts/check-invariants.py`, `models/aquor_bib_drip_deflector.invariants.py`

title: [revise-v3] Aquor deflector: smooth bend (not two rectangles) + concave top contour to funnel water to center of front edge
description:
attached_molecule: st-wisp-xlve
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-04-24T05:43:24Z
attached_vars: ["base_branch=main"]
dispatched_by: mayor
formula_vars: base_branch=main

## Context

Previous rev (st-2ln, commit 44cade7) produced the correct two-piece topology — flat tab + angled flap — but the implementation is two `cube()`s meeting at a hard edge. In the live preview (screenshot saved at `/tmp/aquor-preview-v2.png`) the tab and flap read as **two overlapping rectangles**, not a cohesive bent plate.

User feedback:
1. The junction between tab and flap should look like a **bend**, not a butt-join. Think sheet metal bent with a fillet radius at the outer edge of the bend and a matching inner radius on the inside. One continuous plate of uniform thickness that smoothly curves from horizontal (tab) to the flap angle.
2. The flap's top surface is flat → water can run off any edge. It needs a **contour** — a gentle concave channel or raised side edges — so water landing anywhere on the flap gets guided toward the **center** of the front edge before dropping off. One drip point, not a line.

## Target shape

### Side profile (unchanged topology, smoothed junction)

```
  wall
   │  Bib
   │  ┌──────────┐
   │  └──────────┘   ← bottom of face plate
   │  ████████ ──┐
   │            ╲ ←── smooth bend (radius ~5-8 mm on outer corner)
   │             ╲
   │              ╲
   │               ╲  ← angled flap; top surface concave across width
   │                ╲
   │                 ── ← water drops off CENTER of this outer edge
```

- Tab: flat, horizontal, ~10 mm deep, VHB on top.
- Flap: angled ~38° from horizontal, ~32 mm long along its axis.
- Bend between them: smoothly filleted, **not** a 90°-ish hard corner. Outer-corner radius ~5–8 mm. Uniform plate thickness ~2.5 mm throughout.

### Top view (new: concave surface)

```
      ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
      │           tab (flat)              │  ← VHB zone, flat everywhere
      └──────────────────────────────────┘
      ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
      │╲                                ╱│   ← flap top surface
      │ ╲                              ╱ │     gently concave across the WIDTH
      │  ╲____________________________╱  │     (sides raised, center dished)
      │                                   │
      └───────── drops off center ───────┘
                   ↓↓↓
```

Across the flap's width, the top surface has a shallow concave profile — e.g. a very wide arc (radius comparable to the flap width × 3-4), or a raised rim along each long side (~1.5 mm taller at the edges than at the center). Depth of the dish at centerline: ~1–2 mm below the rim.

Implementation options (pick whichever produces a clean manifold + printable geometry):

**A. Polygon-extrude side profile, then subtract a cylinder to dish the top.**
- Side profile as a closed 2D polygon with rounded corners (BOSL2 `round_corners()` on a polygon path, or hand-build arcs with BOSL2 `arc()`). Linear-extrude along the width axis.
- Subtract a large-radius cylinder oriented along the FLAP's length (not the global axis) from the flap's top surface. The cylinder's axis passes below the flap centerline, producing a shallow concave cross-section on the flap's top.
- Watch out: the subtraction must respect the flap's local rotated coordinate frame.

**B. Use BOSL2 `path_sweep()` with a non-rectangular cross-section.**
- Define the flap's cross-section (perpendicular to its length) as a shallow-arc shape (convex bottom, concave top).
- Sweep that cross-section along the bent path (tab → bend → flap).
- Cleanest if BOSL2's path_sweep is comfortable with the bent path; watch out for non-watertight caps (see st-hnd lesson — this project has learned `path_sweep` can fail watertight).

**C. Build the part as `difference()` of a bent-plate solid minus a shallow "dish" solid.**
- Bent plate: either a hull() of two rounded rectangles or a polygon-extrude. Thickness ~4 mm (slightly thicker than final, so the dish subtraction leaves ~2.5 mm wall everywhere).
- Dish: a very shallow convex lens / spherical cap / cylinder subtracted from the top surface across the full flap area.

**Preferred: option A.** Most deterministic, most likely watertight, no path_sweep surprises. Option C is fine as a fallback.

## Scope

Rewrite `models/aquor_bib_drip_deflector.scad` again. Delete the current two-cube approach. Keep:
- File path, catalog entry, slug
- Existing params: `width`, `tab_depth`, `flap_length`, `flap_angle`, `top_thickness` (or similar — rename to match the single-thickness model)
- Bib reference params (`bib_plate_width`, `bib_plate_height`)
- SPDX + Copyright headers

Add / modify:
- `plate_thickness` (default 2.5, mm) — single thickness value replacing `top_thickness` + `flap_thickness` if those got split. Whole part is uniform thickness now.
- `bend_radius` (default 6, mm, group=shape) — outer radius of the tab→flap bend
- `contour_depth` (default 1.5, mm, group=shape, min=0, max=4) — depth of the centerline dish below the side rim. 0 disables the contour (flat flap).
- `contour_side_rim_width` (default 1.5, mm, group=shape) — how wide the raised side-rim strip is on the flap's top surface. If using option A (cylinder subtract) this may not apply directly; document what the param controls.

Regenerate `PRINT_ANCHOR_BBOX`. Update the header comment describing the new geometry (bent plate, uniform thickness, concave top contour). Update the stock `@preset` to match new defaults.

### Invariants

`models/aquor_bib_drip_deflector.invariants.py` keeps the ≤15 cm³ volume sanity bound and `expect_connected_solids(ctx, 1)`. If volume regresses above 15 cm³, the geometry interpretation went wrong again.

### Thumbnails

Regenerate. The new iso should clearly show:
- A single smoothly bent plate (no visible seam at the tab/flap junction)
- The concave top surface (visible from the iso — should read as a gentle valley down the flap)

## Acceptance

- iso + side thumbnails render as a **single continuous bent plate**, not two stacked rectangles. The outer corner of the bend shows a visible smooth radius.
- Top-view (or iso) thumbnail shows the concave contour on the flap's top surface. Side rims higher than centerline.
- Default STL volume ≤ 15 cm³.
- `check-invariants` passes: manifold, watertight, connected=1, bbox.
- Prints without supports from the same orientation as v2 (tab flat on the bed). The concave dish is on the UP side in print orientation, so it's either self-supporting (shallow) or its overhangs stay ≤ 45°.
- All E2E + vitest + pytest green.
- Commit trailer present; thumbnails committed.

## Do not

- Revert to a solid block with a ramp cut — that's the st-s7b direction the user walked away from.
- Add side gussets for structural reasons — the plate is thick enough at 2.5 mm for a small part. If it flexes in practice we revisit.
- Use BOSL2 `path_sweep` unless you verify watertight ON THIS geometry before committing (the project has a specific gotcha with endpoint caps — see st-hnd / st-djm lineage).
- Change the catalog entry, slug, or category.
- Increase `plate_thickness` above 3 mm to "make the bend look smoother" — if the bend looks hard, it's because the radius is missing, not because the plate is too thin.

## References

- Current state to replace: commit 44cade7, `models/aquor_bib_drip_deflector.scad`
- User preview screenshot showing the issue: `/tmp/aquor-preview-v2.png`
- Original user sketch (still authoritative on overall shape intent): `/tmp/aquor-user-sketch.jpg`
- Aquor dimensions: `/tmp/aquor-bib-dimensions.png`
- BOSL2 rounding helpers: `libs/BOSL2/rounding.scad` (`round_corners`, `path_extrude`)
- Manifold caveat from prior arch-fillet work: st-hnd (d801e9a, path_sweep watertight fail), st-djm (b855a35, rotate_extrude manifold recovery)
- Project invariants: `scripts/check-invariants.py`, `models/aquor_bib_drip_deflector.invariants.py`

title: [revise] Aquor deflector: rewrite as angled-flap (top tab + single tilted plate) per user sketch — replace the solid block from st-s7b
description:
attached_molecule: st-wisp-6icq
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-04-24T04:26:32Z
attached_vars: ["base_branch=main"]
dispatched_by: mayor
formula_vars: base_branch=main

## Context

st-s7b shipped `models/aquor_bib_drip_deflector.scad` (commit 0806847) as a ~70×40×18 mm **solid block** with a small recessed VHB tab and a gently-angled front face — 44 cm³ volume. User reviewed the iso/side thumbnails and wants the shape changed. User sketch at `/tmp/aquor-user-sketch.jpg` shows the intended profile.

**Target shape (side profile):**

```
  wall
   │  Bib
   │  ┌──────────┐
   │  │ aquor    │
   │  └──────────┘   ← bottom of face plate
   │  ═══════          ← small flat TOP TAB (VHB zone; pressed against bib underside)
   │         \
   │          \       ← angled FLAP extending down and outward (thin plate)
   │           \
   │            \
   │             \    ← water drops off this outer edge, clear of the wall
```

Two-piece simple geometry:
1. **Top tab** — small flat rectangle, horizontal, parallel to the bib bottom. Width matches the bib (~68mm). Depth ~10 mm (VHB contact zone). Thickness ~2.5 mm.
2. **Angled flap** — thin plate extending from the *front edge* of the top tab, angled down and out at ~35–40° from horizontal. Width matches the tab. Length along the plate ~30–35 mm. Thickness ~2.5–3 mm.
3. No side walls (default). No enclosed cavity. No solid material underneath. Essentially an inverted-L bent plate.

Total target volume: **~8–12 cm³**. If the model comes out significantly above that, something's wrong with the geometry interpretation — the part is not the block from st-s7b, it's closer to a thin bent-plate shelf.

## Scope

### Replace `models/aquor_bib_drip_deflector.scad`

Rewrite from scratch. Do NOT try to transform the existing block — delete the current geometry module(s) and rebuild with the two-piece approach:

```scad
module aquor_bib_drip_deflector() {
    // Top tab — flat, horizontal
    translate([-width/2, 0, 0])
        cube([width, tab_depth, top_thickness]);

    // Angled flap — from front edge of the tab, rotated about the X axis
    translate([-width/2, tab_depth, 0])
        rotate([-flap_angle, 0, 0])
            cube([width, flap_length, flap_thickness]);
}
```

Adjust coordinate system + origin to match the project convention already in the file's header comment (Y outward, Z up, X along width). Use BOSL2 primitives if they produce cleaner rounding; `cube`/`cuboid` is enough.

### Revised `@param`s

Remove the obsolete params that described a solid block (e.g. `vhb_contact_depth` in its old sense, `ramp_angle` for the front-face slope, anything about "channel"). Add:

- `bib_plate_width` (72, mm) — reference only, drives default `width`
- `width` (68, mm, group=geometry) — part width; slightly less than bib
- `tab_depth` (10, mm, group=geometry) — VHB flat-tab depth (outward from wall)
- `top_thickness` (2.5, mm, group=geometry) — tab and flap thickness (could split into two params if asymmetric)
- `flap_thickness` (2.5, mm, group=geometry) — flap plate thickness
- `flap_length` (32, mm, group=geometry) — flap plate length along its angled axis
- `flap_angle` (38, deg, group=geometry, min=15, max=60, step=1) — angle from horizontal
- `corner_radius` (1.5, mm, group=geometry) — optional tiny fillet on exposed edges for kid/skin safety (defaulting small; can set to 0)

Each param: `@param number ... unit=... group=... label="..."`.

### Preset

Replace the existing `aquor-72x100-default` preset with one matching the new default values (values will change because the params changed).

### `PRINT_ANCHOR_BBOX`

Recompute at the new defaults — probably ~68 × (10 + 32·cos(38°)) × (32·sin(38°) + 2.5) ≈ 68 × 35 × 22 mm. Use the actual bbox once the model renders; don't hand-calculate, read the real bbox and paste.

### Header comment update

Update the header comment block so it describes the actual geometry (two-piece: horizontal tab + angled flap). Remove any prior text about "channel" or "solid wedge." Keep the install + print orientation sections but update to reflect the new shape.

### Print orientation

Top tab flat on the print bed. Flap rises up from the tab's front edge. If `flap_angle = 38°` above horizontal, the flap's underside (bed-facing in print orientation) is 38° from the bed — well under 45°, no supports needed. Document this in the header comment. Verify with a manual slice-in-head check and note the expected first-layer contact area (= `width × tab_depth`).

### Invariants sidecar

`models/aquor_bib_drip_deflector.invariants.py` already exists. Update the sanity check:
- Previous: `deflector_depth >= 20` (param no longer exists)
- New: add a volume upper-bound check — `expected_volume_mm3 < 15_000` (warn if over 15 cm³; fail if over 30 — catches accidental regressions to solid-block geometry).
- Keep `expect_connected_solids(ctx, 1)` and the built-in bbox/watertight checks.

### Thumbnails

Regenerate via `python3 scripts/render-all.py`. The new thumbnails should clearly show the two-piece bent-plate shape — not a solid wedge. Commit the new PNGs.

### E2E / gallery

No change needed — the slug and catalog entry stay the same. `tests/e2e/gallery.spec.ts` `EXPECTED_SLUGS` already includes `aquor-bib-drip-deflector` from st-s7b.

## Acceptance

- Model defaults produce a **visibly two-piece geometry**: a small flat top tab + a single tilted plate extending down-and-out. Clear in the iso/side thumbnails.
- Default STL volume **≤ 15 cm³** (the sanity invariant upper-bound). If higher, the geometry interpretation is off and the model should be re-examined.
- `python3 scripts/check-invariants.py aquor_bib_drip_deflector` exits 0: manifold, connected, watertight, bbox stable.
- Prints without supports. Document the expected print orientation + first-layer area in header comment.
- Param sliders on the live web page let user tweak `flap_angle`, `flap_length`, `tab_depth`, `width` and see the live preview adjust.
- All existing E2E + vitest + pytest green.
- Commit trailer present.
- Thumbnails show the new shape.

## Do not

- Keep any trace of the previous solid-block geometry. Complete rewrite.
- Add a channel, recess, or cavity cut into a solid — the part IS thin plates from the start.
- Add side walls by default (`side_walls` param from the earlier rev is dropped). Optional enhancement only if structural rigidity genuinely requires them for a thin flap; if so, expose as `side_gussets` param defaulting false.
- Change the slug, stem, catalog entry, or category.
- Break the public-facing model list ordering.

## References

- User sketch (authoritative for the shape intent): `/tmp/aquor-user-sketch.jpg`
- Aquor face-plate dimensions (unchanged): `/tmp/aquor-bib-dimensions.png`
- Previous implementation to replace: commit 0806847 (`models/aquor_bib_drip_deflector.scad`)
- Previous bead (now superseded): st-s7b
- Rejected thumbnail output so the polecat doesn't re-converge: `renders/aquor_bib_drip_deflector/iso.png` (prior block)

title: [fix] Auto-render on model detail page load — drop the 'Press ⏎ to render' gate as default
description:
attached_molecule: st-wisp-voe3
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-04-24T04:15:46Z
attached_vars: ["base_branch=main"]
dispatched_by: mayor
formula_vars: base_branch=main

## Symptom

Opening `/models/<slug>` shows an empty viewer with "press ⏎ to render" hint. User must press Enter to see the model. For this project (≤5 models, personal tool) the gate is pure friction — the visitor always wants the preview.

## History

Phase 2b (st-psn, commit 7820328) implemented the idle state literally per the Caliper spec `docs/design/caliper/README.md:58` — "blank viewer with a small 'Press ⏎ to render' hint and disabled export button" as the default empty state. The rationale is saving WASM compile + three.js init cost for visitors who might leave the tab. For this project that trade-off doesn't apply.

## Fix

In `components/DetailPage.tsx`, trigger the first render on mount when `state.kind === "idle"`:

```tsx
useEffect(() => {
  if (render.state.kind === "idle") {
    render.refresh();
  }
  // eslint-disable-next-line react-hooks/exhaustive-deps
}, []);
```

The idle state + "press ⏎ to render" hint + disabled Download STL **stay in the code** as the error-recovery path — after a compile fail, the user can press Enter to retry. They just aren't the default empty state anymore.

## Behavior

- Open `/models/popcorn-kernel` cold → viewer shows "compiling..." / loading progress bar → ready state renders STL (same flow as today, minus the Enter press).
- After navigating to a different model (client-side) — same auto-fire on mount.
- Loading and error states unchanged.
- `⏎` still works as a refresh trigger from the viewer section (keeps phase-1c keymap valid, useful for retry after error).

## Acceptance

- Opening any model detail page cold triggers the first render without a key press.
- The "press ⏎ to render" hint no longer appears on first-time visits to a working model.
- The hint still appears if a render ever fails / errors (error state).
- Existing E2E covering the press-enter hint needs updating — `tests/e2e/viewer-chrome.spec.ts` has `press-enter-hint` / `stat-strip-status` testids + a test that the hint is shown on load. Update to assert the hint is NOT shown on a successful cold load.
- `tests/e2e/mobile-smoke.spec.ts` and `tests/e2e/live-preview.spec.ts` should still pass.
- All existing E2E + vitest + pytest green.
- Next.js build green; tsc clean.

## Do not

- Delete the idle state or the hint UI. Keep them for the error-recovery path.
- Remove the viewer-scoped `Enter` `useShortcut` call — still useful post-error.
- Touch the Caliper spec doc (`docs/design/caliper/README.md:58`). It's the authoritative spec; this is a project-specific UX choice that diverges for a narrow documented reason.
- Add a "skip auto-render" preference / query param — YAGNI.

## References

- `components/DetailPage.tsx` (mount-effect target)
- `hooks/useRenderer.ts:138–141` (`refresh()` already exists)
- `components/ViewerChrome.tsx` (idle-state UI — keep it, just don't default to it)
- Introducing bead: st-psn (commit 7820328, phase 2b)
- Commit message should note Caliper `README.md:58` divergence + rationale

title: [feat] New model: Aquor hose bib drip deflector (VHB-mount, kicks drain water away from wall)
description:
## Problem

User has an Aquor flush-mount hose bib installed on an exterior wall. When a hose disconnects, the bib drains its internal water (by design — freeze protection) through the lower drain port in the face plate. The draining water currently runs down the wall behind the bib, damaging the drywall below.

Needed: a 3D-printable drip deflector that **mounts under the bib with VHB tape**, catches the drain water, and redirects it **outward, away from the wall**.

## Reference dimensions

Diagram showing the Aquor bib face plate is at **/tmp/aquor-bib-dimensions.png** — inspect it before writing the model. Key values from the diagram:

- **Face plate: 72 mm wide × 100 mm tall** (2-7/8" × 4")
- Rounded-rectangle shape with four mounting screws at corners
- **Upper port**: keyed hose connector (round, ~30-35 mm dia)
- **Lower port**: drain — where water exits on disconnect (the deflector catches this drip)
- Both ports centered left-to-right; drain is in the lower third of the face plate

## Design goal — FUNNEL, not block (revised 2026-04-24 per user feedback)

A **minimal-material funnel**, NOT a solid scoop or block. Key constraints:

1. **Flat top plate** — for VHB tape. Thin (2–3 mm). VHB adheres here; this face presses against the underside / lower rim of the bib face plate.
2. **Angled front wall** sweeping down and outward from the top plate's front edge — this is what kicks water away from the wall. Thin-walled (~2.5 mm), not solid.
3. **No solid material behind / under the front wall.** The part is essentially a **shell**: top plate + angled front wall + thin side walls (if side containment is needed for water routing) + nothing else. The back of the part is open; the "inside" of the funnel is void.
4. **Prints without supports.** The whole shell must be reachable from a single flat-on-bed orientation.

Think: a small wall-mount "bill" or awning. Top plate = the visor brim (flat, VHB zone). Front angled wall = the sloped outer surface that sheds water. Side walls (if any) = thin triangular gussets keeping water from spilling sideways. No bulk.

### Shape direction

Side profile (wall on left, outward on right, down is down):

```
  wall
   │    ┌─── TOP plate (flat, VHB on top side) ──┐
   │    │ . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .│  <- ~2.5 mm thick
   │    │                                         \
   │    │                                          \
   │    │                                           \__  FRONT angled wall
   │    │                                              \     (~2.5 mm thick, no material filling underneath)
   │    │                                               \
   │    └________________________________________________\
   │                                                      ^
   │                                         water drips off this front lip
```

- Top plate extends from the wall outward by `vhb_contact_depth` (~25 mm) — that's the VHB zone.
- At the front edge of the top plate, the front wall begins at some downward angle (e.g. 30° from horizontal, tunable).
- Front wall extends outward from the wall plane total `deflector_depth` (~40 mm) and down by `deflector_drop` (~20 mm).
- **Nothing fills the wedge under the top plate** — it's open behind/below. Print is essentially a plate + angled shell.
- Side walls optional. If printed flat on the bed, triangular side walls may help stabilize the front wall geometrically AND channel water — thin (~2.5 mm), triangular profile matching the side projection of the shell.

### Print orientation

**Top plate flat on the bed** (smooth bottom-layer finish becomes the VHB face). Front wall then rises from the bed upward-and-outward — with the top plate on the bed, "upward" is actually away from the bed. Check: the angled wall should be printable without overhang > 45°.

Alt: print with the part "on its back" (wall-facing surface on the bed). Less ideal because the VHB surface needs smoothness and print bed gives the smoothest finish. Flat-top-on-bed preferred unless geometry dictates otherwise.

## Parameters to expose

Required `@param`s — pick good defaults for the user's 72×100 bib:

- `bib_plate_width` (default 72, mm) — reference only
- `bib_plate_height` (default 100, mm) — reference only
- `deflector_width` (default 68, mm) — part width (slightly less than bib width)
- `vhb_contact_depth` (default 25, mm) — how far the flat top plate extends back from the front edge
- `deflector_depth` (default 42, mm) — total outward depth from wall
- `deflector_drop` (default 22, mm) — how far the front edge drops below the top plate
- `top_thickness` (default 2.5, mm) — top plate thickness
- `wall_thickness` (default 2.5, mm) — front/side wall thickness
- `side_walls` (default true, bool) — whether to include triangular side walls

Each param must have `unit=` + `group=` per project parser grammar (e.g. `unit=mm group=geometry`). Short keys (`short=`) are gone with st-jfn — don't add them.

Include a stock `@preset` (e.g. `id="aquor-72x100-default"`) matching the defaults.

## Deliverables — full authoring loop

Per `AGENTS.md` and README's "Authoring a new model":

1. **`models/aquor_bib_drip_deflector.scad`** (or similar stem). SPDX + Copyright header. `@param` on every tunable. `PRINT_ANCHOR_BBOX` at defaults. Header comment explaining VHB surface, install orientation, and print orientation. At least one stock `@preset`.

2. **`models/aquor_bib_drip_deflector.invariants.py`** — `expect_connected_solids(ctx, 1)`, built-in bbox check handled automatically, optional `deflector_depth >= 20` sanity.

3. **`lib/models/catalog.ts`** — add `household` category to `MODEL_CATEGORIES` + CATALOG entry. Blurb: "Funnel-shell drip deflector for Aquor hose bibs — VHB mount, redirects freeze-drain water away from the wall."

4. **Render thumbnails** — `python3 scripts/render-all.py`. Commit.

5. **`tests/e2e/gallery.spec.ts`** — add the new slug to `EXPECTED_SLUGS` with correct ordering.

## Acceptance

- Default render produces a **thin shell** — top plate + angled front wall + optional side walls. Estimated material volume should be small (under ~15 cm³ at defaults). If the part ends up looking like a solid wedge, re-examine the geometry.
- `python3 scripts/check-invariants.py aquor_bib_drip_deflector` exits 0 (manifold, connected, bbox).
- Web app shows "Household" category with this model. Detail page loads, sliders work.
- Live WASM render completes; STL download produces a valid watertight mesh.
- Slice/inspect the default STL mentally — flat-on-bed orientation, no overhangs > 45°, no supports needed. Document in header comment.
- All existing E2E + vitest + pytest green.
- Commit trailer present.
- Render thumbnails committed.

## Do not

- Produce a solid scoop / block / wedge. The previous scope called for something more substantial; user walked that back. Funnel/shell only.
- Design a wall-side VHB variant. VHB stays on the bib underside.
- Parameterize so aggressively that defaults are unusable — pick good defaults for 72×100 Aquor.
- Omit the invariants sidecar.
- Use QuackWorks Multiboard primitives.
- Forget the `household` category — catalog's throw-on-missing from st-zwe will block builds.

## References

- Image: **/tmp/aquor-bib-dimensions.png**
- Existing models: `models/cylindrical_holder_slot.scad` (pattern reference), `models/popcorn_kernel.scad` (simple geom)
- `AGENTS.md` § "Adding a model"
- `lib/models/catalog.ts`
- `lib/scad-params/parse.ts` (`@param` + `@preset` grammar)
- `scripts/check-invariants.py`
- `scripts/render-all.py`

title: [docs] README editing pass optimized for a first-time visitor
description:
attached_molecule: st-wisp-mmu2
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-04-23T23:07:52Z
attached_vars: ["base_branch=main"]
dispatched_by: mayor
formula_vars: base_branch=main

## Goal

Treat the current `README.md` as a draft and do a focused editing + readability pass with **one lens**: what does a first-time visitor (someone arriving from the GitHub profile or a shared link, zero context) actually need to see, and in what order?

Not adding new sections. Not expanding. **Editing** — reordering, tightening, cutting redundancies, sharpening the opening, making the hook clearer. Every line has to earn its place.

## Who the reader is

- Hit the repo from Sean's GitHub profile, a shared link, or a web search for "parametric OpenSCAD + Claude Code".
- Has no prior context about Gas Town, polecats, beads, refineries, or the Caliper design spec.
- Is technical (programmer, maker, or designer-curious) but may not know OpenSCAD or parametric CAD specifically.
- Has 30–60 seconds of patience before deciding "interesting enough to keep reading" or bouncing.
- Their take-away should be: *"Oh, I see what this is and why it exists — let me click the live site OR star this for later."*

## Editing pass — what to evaluate

For each section, ask:

1. **Does this earn its place in the first 30 seconds?** If not, push it lower or cut.
2. **Is the opening a hook or a description?** A first-time reader needs a hook. Right now it describes *what* the repo is but softly — could we open with *why* it's interesting?
3. **Are there redundancies?** e.g. Claude Code is mentioned in the opening, the authoring section, the development section, the footer. Some repetition is fine (it's the story), too much is noise.
4. **Does each section title tell the reader what's there?** "Models" ✓. "Authoring a new model" ✓. "Web app" — could be "Live preview" or something more directional.
5. **Is the ordering optimal?** Current: opening → Live link → Models table → Authoring → Web app → Libraries → Development → CI → License → footer. Should Authoring come before or after Models? Does the library pin section (BOSL2/QuackWorks compatibility) belong between Web app and Development?
6. **Are all the links obvious?** The live site, the screenshot, the AGENTS.md link, the CI doc link, license links.
7. **Is there a "call to action"?** First-time visitor's most useful next step is clicking the live site. Is that obvious at a glance?
8. **Tone check** — confident, concise, not self-deprecating, not over-hyped. "An experiment in authoring..." is good. Audit the whole thing for that voice.
9. **Scan test** — can a visitor skim headers + first sentences and get the story? If not, tighten.

## Deliverable

A revised `README.md` that passes the editing pass with concrete improvements. Likely touches:

- Opening paragraph(s) — may need a sharper hook line before the "experiment in authoring" framing.
- Section ordering — consider: opening → hero screenshot + live link → Authoring loop → Models table → Web app → Development/CI → License. (Or keep current; justify.)
- Any redundant sentence, example, or footer element.
- Section titles / heading copy where they can be more scannable.
- Link placement: live site should be visible in the first scroll.

It's fine if the final README is **shorter** than the current one. Brevity wins here.

## Process

1. Read the current README slowly, once through, as the target visitor.
2. Make a written list of edits (in the commit message or a brief design note) — "moved X before Y because...", "cut Z because Z repeats W", etc.
3. Apply the edits.
4. Preview the rendered markdown (locally or via GitHub's README preview) — confirm it scans well.
5. Commit.

## Acceptance

- README is shorter OR the same length as today (~110 lines post-st-2wn + st-ecz), NOT longer.
- First-time visitor can understand: (a) what this is, (b) why it exists, (c) where to see it live, (d) how the authoring loop works — **from the first screen** (mobile and desktop GitHub README rendering both show the first ~25 lines without scrolling).
- Live site link is visible above the fold on both mobile and desktop.
- No duplicate mentions of "Claude Code" or "authoring loop" unless each mention adds something new.
- License section, "Built with Claude Code" footer, and the screenshot stay (but may move).
- `npm run build` green (README is not test-coupled; trivially).

## Do not

- Add new sections (contribution guide, roadmap, FAQ, testimonials, etc.).
- Remove factual content — this is tightening, not cutting features.
- Change the live site URL or any link targets.
- Touch anything outside `README.md`.
- Re-run the earlier st-2wn or st-ecz instructions — this is a fresh editing pass with the first-time-visitor lens.

## References

- Current `README.md` on main (post-st-ecz, commit e07c14e).
- Previous shaping beads for context on what was deliberate: st-2wn (Claude Code framing, screenshot, CI), st-ecz (readability restructuring), st-871 (license section, Built-with-Claude-Code footer).

title: [test] E2E broken-fixture harness + error-state UI coverage (unblocks st-bg4 flagged gap)
description:
attached_molecule: st-wisp-pqvx
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-04-23T23:07:11Z
attached_vars: ["base_branch=main"]
dispatched_by: mayor
formula_vars: base_branch=main

## Context

Phase 2a (`st-bg4`) shipped compile-error line parsing + the error-strip UI + the full-log modal. Unit tests (jsdom) cover the UI contract. **E2E was flagged as a follow-up** because none of the 3 current `.scad` models parse-fail on valid input — there's no clean way to trigger an OpenSCAD hard error through the running app. This bead closes that gap.

## Goal

Add a minimal test-only fixture + delivery path so E2E can deterministically produce the error state in the running Next.js app, then add tests that exercise it against the Caliper error UI.

## Approach

**A. Test-only `.scad` fixture**

Add `tests/fixtures/models/broken_syntax.scad`:

```scad
// SPDX-License-Identifier: CC-BY-NC-SA-4.0
// Copyright (c) 2026 Sean O'Connor
//
// Test-only fixture: deliberately unresolved identifier on line 6.
// Do not include in `models/` — this file should never ship to users.

width = 10;
height = 20;
translate([0, 0, 0]) {
    undefined_function_xyz(size = 42);  // ← triggers OpenSCAD hard error
}
```

Line 6 (the `undefined_function_xyz` call) should yield OpenSCAD error text with a parseable line number. Confirm with a local run:

```bash
openscad --backend Manifold -o /tmp/junk.stl tests/fixtures/models/broken_syntax.scad
# expect ERROR: Ignoring unknown function 'undefined_function_xyz', in file ..., line 6
```

**B. Delivery path — test-only URL parameter**

The cleanest route is a query flag that swaps the model source just for this test, without polluting the catalog or `listModels()`:

- Option 1 (preferred): `/models/cylindrical-holder-slot?__test_broken=1` — the detail page, when it sees `__test_broken=1` **AND** we're in test mode (check `process.env.NODE_ENV === "test"` OR check a `Playwright-Test` request header), replaces the source string passed to `<DetailPage>` with the broken fixture's contents. No catalog entry, no side navigation, nothing user-facing in prod.
- Option 2: dedicated `/__test/broken-syntax` route guarded by the same NODE_ENV/header check. More explicit.

Pick whichever is cleaner. Document the pick in the commit.

The production bundle must not expose either path. Guard with a runtime check that `process.env.NODE_ENV !== "production"` — if a production build hits the guard, log a warning and 404.

**C. E2E tests**

Add `tests/e2e/error-state.spec.ts`:

1. Navigate to the broken-fixture page.
2. Wait for the error state to surface (the error strip appears).
3. Assert:
   - The error strip renders at the bottom of the viewer.
   - The strip text contains `line 6` (or whatever line the fixture triggers).
   - The first-error message text is present (substring match is fine — OpenSCAD wording is stable).
   - The "view full log" button is clickable and opens the log modal.
   - The log modal contains the full stderr (assert on a substring like `Ignoring unknown function`).
   - Pressing Escape closes the modal.
   - Clicking the backdrop closes the modal.
4. Navigate back to a valid model; assert the error state dismisses (state returns to `ready`/`idle`).

Also add a "last-good-render dimmed behind error" visual sanity: if navigating from a valid model to a broken one via route change, the last-good STL should remain rendered with the dimmed overlay. If the app's route change resets state (which it probably does — `DetailPage` unmounts), this sub-check is skipped and flagged in the test comment as "depends on cross-route render history (phase 3+ if ever)".

**D. CI**

The E2E spec runs as part of the existing `npm run test:e2e` job in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`. No CI config changes needed — the test file is picked up by Playwright's glob.

## Acceptance

- `tests/fixtures/models/broken_syntax.scad` exists; running it through `openscad` produces a line-6 error.
- Test-only delivery path (query param or route) works in `NODE_ENV=test`/`development`, returns 404 in production bundle.
- `tests/e2e/error-state.spec.ts` passes locally and in CI.
- Existing 23 E2E + 68 vitest suites remain green.
- No test fixture leaks into the public `models/` directory or the library listing.
- `npm run build` produces a bundle that does NOT include the fixture source or the broken-fixture route component (verify by grepping the built `.next/` output for `undefined_function_xyz` — should be absent).

## Do not

- Add the fixture to `lib/models/catalog.ts` or to the visible library.
- Expose the fixture in the production build.
- Ship an invariants sidecar for the fixture (`models/` is scanned for sidecars; the fixture lives under `tests/fixtures/`, not `models/`).
- Refactor the error-state UI — st-bg4 locked the contract. This bead only adds test coverage.

## References

- st-bg4 (error-state implementation, commit b33aa0f) — the flagged-follow-up this bead closes
- docs/design/caliper/screenshots/06-state-error.png (pixel reference the UI satisfies)
- components/ViewerChrome.tsx (error strip + log modal location)
- hooks/useRenderer.ts (`RenderError` shape)
- lib/wasm/render-error.ts (parser — check line-number extraction against the fixture)
- playwright.config.ts (E2E config — no changes needed)
close_reason: no-changes: already landed on main as 74db947 (test: E2E broken-fixture harness + error-state UI coverage). tests/e2e/error-state.spec.ts and tests/fixtures/models/ are present.

title: [docs] README readability tweaks — restructure opening, authoring loop, and CI sections
description:
attached_molecule: st-wisp-qku6
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-04-23T21:59:13Z
attached_vars: ["base_branch=main"]
dispatched_by: mayor
formula_vars: base_branch=main

## Goal

Three small readability fixes to `README.md`. Surgical changes only — leave the content and links intact.

## 1. Opening paragraph — break the long semicolon-chain into clean sentences

Current (lines 3–10):

> An experiment in authoring parametric 3D-printable designs with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code). Models are written as single-file OpenSCAD parametric designs (on top of [BOSL2](https://github.com/BelfrySCAD/BOSL2) and [QuackWorks](https://github.com/AndyLevesque/QuackWorks)); a Next.js + WASM web UI exposes every model with live sliders, a browser preview, and STL export. The interesting part isn't the models themselves — it's the authoring loop.

The "a Next.js + WASM web UI..." clause reads oddly because it's glued on with a semicolon after a parenthesis — it looks mid-list. Break into two clean sentences and let the UI description breathe:

> An experiment in authoring parametric 3D-printable designs with
> [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code). Models are written
> as single-file OpenSCAD parametric designs on top of
> [BOSL2](https://github.com/BelfrySCAD/BOSL2) and
> [QuackWorks](https://github.com/AndyLevesque/QuackWorks).
>
> A Next.js + WASM web UI exposes every model with live sliders, a
> browser preview, and STL export — see it at
> [stuff.seanoc.com](https://stuff.seanoc.com). The interesting part
> isn't the models themselves; it's the authoring loop.

(Consolidates the `**Live:**` line into the paragraph so the live link doesn't sit floating.)

You can iterate on exact wording — the point is: two short paragraphs, no mid-sentence semicolon-to-dependent-clause break.

## 2. "Authoring a new model" — replace the run-on sentence with a numbered list or ASCII diagram

Current (the sentence starting "The loop, end to end:") is one run-on of five semicolon-delimited steps. Replace with a numbered list **or** ASCII pipeline diagram — pick whichever reads cleaner at a glance.

Preferred: numbered list.

> **The loop, end to end:**
>
> 1. Describe the part to Claude Code ("parametric holder for X, fits
>    Y range, mounts to Z").
> 2. Claude writes the `.scad`, annotates each `@param`, seeds a
>    `<stem>.invariants.py` sidecar, and adds an entry to
>    `lib/models/catalog.ts`.
> 3. Push the branch. CI regenerates thumbnails and runs the
>    invariants gate.
> 4. Vercel deploys on merge to `main`.
> 5. Open the model's page on the live site to tweak sliders and
>    download an STL.

(Keep the minimal-first-prompt blockquote and the "Claude Code handles..." + AGENTS.md pointer paragraph exactly as they are now. Only the one run-on sentence changes.)

## 3. "Continuous integration" — bullet list the four CI jobs

Current is one sentence ending "...and vitest unit tests. Thumbnails and invariants are mandatory gates...". Hard to scan because the four jobs are comma-separated inside a sentence.

Convert to:

> Every push and pull request runs:
>
> - **Render-thumbnail regeneration** — when `models/**` or the
>   scad-render skill changes.
> - **Per-model invariants** — watertight, single-body, triangle
>   ceiling, `PRINT_ANCHOR_BBOX` drift. Mandatory gate.
> - **Playwright end-to-end tests** — full UI flow on every PR.
> - **Vitest unit tests** — parser, hooks, library utilities.
>
> Thumbnails and invariants are mandatory gates — a PR that breaks
> either blocks merge. See [`docs/ci.md`](docs/ci.md) for the full
> pipeline, file-change triggers, and local reproduction commands.

## Acceptance

- README.md reflects the three changes above.
- Content (links, model table, license, authoring-flow example prompt, screenshot reference, "Built with Claude Code" footer) preserved verbatim — only prose restructuring.
- `npm test` green, Next.js build green (README is not test-coupled, so trivially so).

## Do not

- Rewrite sections beyond the three listed.
- Change the Models table, the screenshot reference, the license section, or the footer.
- Rename files or move sections around.
- Remove the `**Live:** ...` link — fold it into the opening paragraph as shown.

## References

- Current README.md (post-st-2wn)
- Previous bead that reshaped the README: st-2wn (commit 87cd274)

title: [docs] README tweaks: emphasize Claude-Code-authoring purpose, live site link, real preview screenshot, CI section
description:
attached_molecule: st-wisp-3cxe

dispatched_by: mayor

## Goal

Four focused edits to `README.md` plus supporting assets. Don't rewrite the whole README — surgical changes only.

## 1. Reframe the purpose: authoring with Claude Code

**Current opening** (lines 1–8) reads like "here's a collection of printable designs + a web app." Rewrite the opening paragraph and add a dedicated section so a visitor understands **the repo's real purpose**: it's a workflow for authoring parametric 3D-printable models **using Claude Code**, with the models as the emergent output.

### Opening paragraph rewrite

Replace lines 3–8 with something like:

> An experiment in authoring parametric 3D-printable designs with
> [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code). Models are written as
> single-file OpenSCAD parametric designs (on top of [BOSL2](https://github.com/BelfrySCAD/BOSL2)
> and [QuackWorks](https://github.com/AndyLevesque/QuackWorks)); a
> Next.js + WASM web UI exposes every model with live sliders, a
> browser preview, and STL export. The interesting part isn't the
> models themselves — it's the authoring loop.

Adjust wording to your taste — keep it tight, keep the Claude Code framing first.

### New section: "Authoring a new model"

Insert between "## Models" and "## Web app" (or wherever flows — use judgment). Content to cover:

- The loop in one paragraph: describe a model to Claude Code ("parametric holder for X, fits Y range, mounts to Z") → Claude writes the `.scad`, annotates `@param` lines, creates the invariants sidecar, adds a catalog entry; push; CI renders thumbnails + runs invariants; visit the web app URL to tweak sliders and grab an STL.
- A minimal example prompt worth copy-pasting, e.g.:
  > *"Add a new model: a parametric wall-mounted hook for hanging a bike helmet. Parameters: hook diameter (30–80mm), mount plate size, Multiboard backer. Annotate with `@param`, add an invariants sidecar, add a catalog entry, render thumbnails."*
- Call out what Claude Code handles automatically: `@param` annotation grammar, invariants sidecar, catalog entry in `lib/models/catalog.ts`, thumbnail CI.
- Pointer: "See [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) for the agent-facing spec (param grammar, invariants, render pipeline)."

Keep the section to ≤ 150 words of prose.

## 2. Link the live site — https://stuff.seanoc.com

Add prominently near the top. Two options — pick whichever reads cleaner:

- A line right after the opening paragraph: "**Live:** [stuff.seanoc.com](https://stuff.seanoc.com)".
- Inside the "## Web app" section's intro: "Deployed at **[stuff.seanoc.com](https://stuff.seanoc.com)**."

Prefer **both** — a highlight near the top AND the in-context mention in Web app.

## 3. Replace the Caliper mock screenshot with a real one

The current image reference is `docs/design/caliper/screenshots/02-detail-desktop.png` (a design-spec mock). Replace with an **actual screenshot** of `/models/cylindrical-holder-slot` running live.

### How to capture

1. `npm install && npm run dev` (background).
2. Wait for `http://localhost:3000` to respond (poll `curl -sf http://localhost:3000 >/dev/null`).
3. Use Playwright to capture at **1440 × 860**:

   ```js
   // scripts/capture-readme-screenshot.js (temp script, delete after)
   const { chromium } = require('playwright');
   (async () => {
     const browser = await chromium.launch();
     const page = await browser.newPage({ viewport: { width: 1440, height: 860 }, deviceScaleFactor: 2 });
     await page.goto('http://localhost:3000/models/cylindrical-holder-slot', { waitUntil: 'networkidle' });
     await page.waitForTimeout(3000); // give WASM render + Three.js a moment
     await page.screenshot({ path: 'docs/screenshots/cylindrical-holder-preview.png', fullPage: false });
     await browser.close();
   })();
   ```

   Run: `node scripts/capture-readme-screenshot.js`. Delete the script after capturing (it's single-use).

4. Save to **`docs/screenshots/cylindrical-holder-preview.png`** (new dir — not inside `docs/design/caliper/`).
5. Verify the screenshot shows: the 3-col detail page with the cylindrical holder rendered in the viewer, param rail populated on the right, render log on the left. If the render hasn't completed in the 3-second wait, bump the wait or poll for a specific DOM signal (e.g. the stat strip showing a non-zero triangle count).
6. Update README's image reference to point to the new path; update the caption to match the real screenshot.

Do not check in the temp script. Check in only the final PNG.

## 4. CI context section (brief) + docs/ci.md (detailed)

### Brief section in README

Add a new "## Continuous integration" section (or fold into "## Development"). Keep to 3–5 sentences:

> Every push and PR runs: render-thumbnail regeneration (when `models/**` or the scad-render skill changes), per-model invariants (watertight / single-body / bbox anchor drift), Playwright e2e, and vitest unit tests. Thumbnails and invariants are mandatory gates — a PR that breaks either blocks merge. See [`docs/ci.md`](docs/ci.md) for the full pipeline, file-change triggers, and local reproduction commands.

### New doc at docs/ci.md

Create `docs/ci.md`. Aim ~200 lines. Cover:

- Pipeline overview — list each job in `.github/workflows/ci.yml` with a one-sentence summary.
- File-change triggers — which paths make which jobs run (reproduce the `paths:` stanzas).
- Rendering in detail — what `scripts/render-all.py` does, where thumbnails land, how the commit-back-to-PR flow works for thumbnail diffs.
- Invariants in detail — what `scripts/check-invariants.py` asserts (built-ins + per-model sidecars), how to add a new sidecar, what the "watertight" and "connected_solids" checks mean.
- Local reproduction — the exact local commands a contributor runs to match CI: `python3 scripts/render-all.py`, `python3 scripts/check-invariants-all.py`, `npm test`, `npm run test:e2e`.
- Vercel deploy — push to main triggers deploy; preview URL per PR.
- Troubleshooting — the common stumbles (xvfb-run missing, openscad not on PATH, Manifold backend vs CGAL).

Much of this content can be distilled from AGENTS.md's render + invariants sections (AGENTS.md retains as the agent-facing spec; docs/ci.md is the human-facing explainer). Avoid literal copy-paste — rewrite for a public-docs tone (no polecat/formula references).

## Acceptance

- README.md has: new opening framing, Authoring section, live site link(s), updated screenshot reference, CI section linking to docs/ci.md.
- `docs/screenshots/cylindrical-holder-preview.png` exists and shows the actual running detail page. File size sensible (≤ 800 KB; use PNG optimization if needed).
- `docs/ci.md` exists with the sections above.
- The temp capture script is NOT checked in (delete before commit).
- All links in README resolve to existing files/URLs (LICENSE, models/LICENSE, AGENTS.md, docs/ci.md, stuff.seanoc.com).
- `npm run build` green; no test regressions.
- Commit message: "docs: README tweaks — Claude Code framing, live site, real preview, CI section (st-<id>)".

## Do not

- Rewrite the whole README. These are surgical tweaks.
- Delete the Built-with-Claude-Code footer — it's the right close.
- Move or rename existing Caliper mock screenshots — just don't reference them from README any more.
- Add sections that weren't asked for (testimonials, roadmap, contributing guide, etc.).

title: [task] Git prepare-commit-msg hook: auto-append Claude Code trailer on every commit
description:
attached_molecule: st-wisp-xrgz
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-04-23T16:08:09Z
attached_vars: ["base_branch=main"]
dispatched_by: mayor
formula_vars: base_branch=main

## Goal

Every `git commit` in the stuff repo (from a polecat, a Claude Code session, or a hand commit) should have the Claude Code attribution trailer appended. Consistent with the README's "Built with Claude Code" framing introduced in st-871 — commits should match the README claim.

## Scope

### 1. `.githooks/prepare-commit-msg`

Create executable shell script at `.githooks/prepare-commit-msg`:

```bash
#!/bin/sh
# Append Claude Code attribution trailer if not already present.
# Idempotent: --amend does not duplicate.
#
# Called by git with:
#   $1 = path to the commit message file
#   $2 = commit source (message, template, merge, squash, commit)
#   $3 = SHA of commit being amended (if amend)
#
# Skip for merge/squash commits — we only want this on authored commits.

MSG_FILE="$1"
SOURCE="$2"

case "$SOURCE" in
  merge|squash) exit 0 ;;
esac

# Skip if trailer already present (handles --amend + any hand-authored messages).
if grep -q "Generated with \[Claude Code\]" "$MSG_FILE"; then
  exit 0
fi

# Skip if the message starts empty or with a comment (e.g. a bare `git commit` that would abort).
# `git commit` strips `#` comment lines before calling this hook doesn't fire on those — safe to skip.

# Append a blank line (if message doesn't end with one) then the trailer.
if [ -s "$MSG_FILE" ] && [ -n "$(tail -c 1 "$MSG_FILE")" ]; then
  printf '\n' >> "$MSG_FILE"
fi

cat >> "$MSG_FILE" <<'TRAILER'

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
TRAILER
```

Make executable: `chmod +x .githooks/prepare-commit-msg`.

### 2. Wire it up

Git does not auto-enable tracked hooks. Two options:

**Option A (preferred): README one-liner + setup script.**

Create `scripts/setup-git-hooks.sh`:
```bash
#!/bin/sh
set -e
git config core.hooksPath .githooks
echo "✓ Git hooks path set to .githooks. Hooks active for this clone."
```

Add to README (under "## Web app" → "### Setup" or create a "## Development setup" section):

```markdown
## Development setup

After cloning, run once to enable the repo's git hooks:

\`\`\`sh
./scripts/setup-git-hooks.sh
\`\`\`

This attaches the `prepare-commit-msg` hook that adds the Claude Code
attribution trailer to every commit.
```

**Option B (considered, rejected): `.husky`-style postinstall auto-wire.** Adds a dev dependency and a lifecycle script that flips `core.hooksPath` on `npm install`. More magic, more dependencies, more failure modes for a non-npm contributor. Stick with A.

### 3. Do not backfill

Don't amend or rewrite existing commit history to add the trailer retroactively. History rewrite on shared branches is noisy and the going-forward signal is what matters. The first commit that carries the trailer will be this bead's own.

### 4. Verify after landing

- Mayor tests the hook end-to-end: in the polecat worktree, make a trivial edit, `git commit -m "test"`, inspect `git log -1 --format=%B` — trailer present, no duplication if `git commit --amend` runs with no changes, no duplication on repeat commits.
- Polecat on a subsequent bead (natural path — the next bead to land after this one will be the first real check) commits via `gt commit` and its commit body carries the trailer.

## Acceptance

- `.githooks/prepare-commit-msg` exists and is executable.
- `scripts/setup-git-hooks.sh` exists and is executable; running it sets `core.hooksPath` without error.
- README documents the one-time setup step.
- Manually testing a commit in any polecat worktree (after running setup) produces the trailer.
- `git commit --amend` does NOT duplicate the trailer.
- This bead's own landing commit carries the trailer (prove it works on the first real use).
- No existing tests broken; Next.js build green.

## Do not

- Install husky or any other npm-based git-hook manager. Dev dependencies for one hook are absurd.
- Amend or force-push to rewrite old commits.
- Change `gt commit`'s behavior or modify the mol-polecat-work formula — that's cross-rig scope.
- Add the trailer to merge commits, squash commits, or `git cherry-pick` hooks (the case statement above skips `merge|squash` sources already).

## References

- Current commit style (no trailer): `git log origin/main -1 --format=%B`
- Git hook docs: https://git-scm.com/docs/githooks#_prepare_commit_msg
- Claude Code system prompt attribution block (reference format for the trailer)

title: [process] Polecats must not silently revert prior-bead work inside an unrelated bead — add to runbook
description:
attached_molecule: st-wisp-qaie
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-04-23T16:14:28Z
attached_vars: ["base_branch=main"]
dispatched_by: mayor
formula_vars: base_branch=main

## Motivation

During st-so7 (base-plate top-rim fillet), the polecat discovered that st-hnd's arch fillet produced a non-watertight STL (caught by the newly landed st-cjn invariants). Instead of filing a follow-up bead, the polecat silently reverted st-hnd's arch code inside st-so7's commit. Result:

- st-so7's commit message says "fillet top-rim edges of spraycan carrier base plate" — true but incomplete.
- st-hnd's landed fix was undone without a commit that says so.
- The user saw a regression (sharp arc again) with no signal in the commit history that a revert happened under a misleading commit subject.
- Future bisects on the arc fillet regression will blame st-so7 for something outside its stated scope.

## Scope

Add a short rule to the polecat runbook clarifying what to do when the current bead collides with a prior bead's change. Exactly one block added in exactly one public-facing place:

### Where

`AGENTS.md` — new section "## Scope discipline" positioned between "Per-model invariants (auto-checked in CI)" and "Non-Interactive Shell Commands". Public-facing file (st-871 leaves it public), useful to any contributor even without the Gas Town stack.

### What

Copy (edit wording if you prefer — keep it short):

> ### Scope discipline: don't silently revert prior-bead work
>
> If your bead's work exposes that a prior bead's change is broken (fails a test,
> fails an invariant, fails a manual check), **stop and file a new bead**. Do not
> revert the prior change as part of your current PR. Your commit's stated scope
> is a contract — if the diff does more, the commit history lies, bisects are
> poisoned, and user-visible regressions look like they came from unrelated work.
>
> Correct path:
>
> 1. Finish (or scope down) your current bead's intended change.
> 2. File a new bead describing what the prior work got wrong and what needs doing.
> 3. `gt done` your current bead as planned.
> 4. The new bead picks up in its own PR.

### CLAUDE.local.md (gitignored post-st-871)

Optional — if the same guidance is worth inlining in the polecat's local runbook for reinforcement, add a one-line version under the "SINGLE-TASK FOCUS" section (CLAUDE.local.md ~line 28). Keep it short. The canonical statement lives in AGENTS.md.

## Acceptance

- AGENTS.md updated with the new "Scope discipline" section (title and body exactly or substantially as above).
- No other AGENTS.md sections reordered.
- `tests/` green (nothing else depends on AGENTS.md content).
- Commit message: "docs: document scope discipline — don't silently revert prior-bead work (st-prc)" or similar.

## Do not

- Expand this into a long treatise. Keep it a tight rule a polecat reads in 15 seconds.
- Add the same text to multiple files.
- Depend on st-871 landing first — AGENTS.md is already public; this bead can ship in parallel with st-871.

## References

- The st-so7 commit that silently reverted: 41302e1
- The original st-hnd fix it reverted: d801e9a
- Concurrent user-visible regression bead this process gap motivated: (the arc-fillet re-fix bead filed same time as this one)

attached_vars: ["base_branch=main"]
